one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive December 2024 survey that maps the whole LLM-safety landscape into four basic risk areas (value misalignment, robustness to attack, misuse, autonomous AI risks) plus four related areas (agents, interpretability, industry roadmaps, governance), positioning itself as broader than prior alignment- or misuse-only surveys.
key_finding: The central thesis is that LLM safety cannot be addressed by scaling alone and demands a proactive, multifaceted approach integrating technical solutions, ethical considerations, and governance frameworks. The survey's synthesized 'finding' is the taxonomy itself plus the argument that prior surveys are too narrow: they cover single aspects (alignment, or catastrophic misuse) or module-based views (Cui et al. 2024's input/model/toolchain/output modules) that omit robustness-against-attack, catastrophic-misuse, and governance/policy dimensions, which this survey unifies.
core_contribution: A holistic survey of LLM safety from the TJUNLP Lab (Tianjin University) that organizes the field into a two-dimensional taxonomy (Figure 1). Dimension one, 'Basic Areas of LLM Safety', covers four key risk categories: (1) Value Misalignment (social bias, privacy, toxicity, ethics/morality), (2) Robustness to Attack (jailbreaking, red teaming, defense), (3) Misuse (weaponization, misinformation campaigns, deepfakes), and (4) Autonomous AI Risks (instrumental goals, goal misalignment, deception, situational awareness). Dimension two, 'Related Areas to LLM Safety', covers four further topics: Agent Safety (language and embodied agents), Interpretability for LLM Safety, Technology Roadmaps/Strategies in Practice (safety approaches of named industry players), and Governance (proposals, policies, visions). The paper's OWN distinctive contribution, relative to the prior surveys it cites (Cui et al. 2024, Chua et al. 2024, Chen et al. 2024a, Ji et al. 2023, Hendrycks et al. 2023), is claimed to be a finer-grained multi-subdomain categorization plus explicit coverage of governance/policy and technology roadmaps that those prior surveys omit. A curated companion paper list is released as a GitHub repo (tjunlp-lab/Awesome-LLM-Safety-Papers).
threat_model: As a survey it catalogs many threat models rather than assuming one. The paper explicitly distinguishes 'safety' (avoiding intended/unintended harms from LLM outputs and misuse) from 'security' (protecting LLM systems from external technical threats like hacking, DoS, data breaches), and scopes itself to safety. Adversarial threat models it reviews include: malicious users bypassing safety guardrails via black-box and white-box jailbreaking; misuse actors leveraging LLMs for cyberattacks, bio/chemical/nuclear weapon acquisition, misinformation campaigns, and deepfakes; privacy adversaries extracting PII via training-data memorization, inference, and privacy attacks; and longer-horizon autonomous-AI risks (self-preservation, power-seeking, deception). Defenses surveyed span external safeguards and internal protections, plus red teaming (manual and automated) as an offensive-evaluation methodology.
limitations: As a broad survey it provides no new experiments, models, or measurements; claims of superiority over prior surveys (finer taxonomy, governance coverage) are asserted qualitatively rather than validated. It is a snapshot fixed at its Dec 2024 arXiv v1 date, so a fast-moving field will outpace it. The safety/security boundary it draws deliberately excludes system-level security (hacking, DoS, data breaches), so readers seeking those must look elsewhere. Coverage breadth also means individual subtopics are treated at overview depth rather than exhaustively.

baselines_compared: Prior LLM-safety surveys contrasted against (not experimental baselines): Cui et al. 2024 (four-module input/language-model/toolchain/output view; noted to omit robustness-to-attack, catastrophic misuse, and governance)
Ji et al. 2023 and Shen et al. 2023 (alignment-focused surveys)
Chua et al. 2024 (working principles + alignment/safety classification)
Chen et al. 2024a (three-pillar framework: Trustworthy AI, Responsible AI, Safe AI)
